coredata.dc:title: Augmented glioma-targeted theranostics using multifunctional polymer-coated carbon nanodots
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Shanshan
Li, Chengyi
Qian, Min
Jiang, Huiling
Shi, Wei
Chen, Jian
Lächelt, Ulrich
Wagner, Ernst
Lu, Weiyue
Wang, Yi
Huang, Rongqin
coredata.dc:description: Overcoming biological barriers to imaging-guided site-specific delivery of therapeutics is the goal of current nanomedicine designs. Here, multifunctional polymer-coated carbon nanodots with an interleukin-6 (IL-6) fragment peptide for receptor-targeting (pCDPI) were prepared for drug delivery. The pCDPI exhibits small hydrodynamic diameters, high water solubility and biocompatibility. In vitro and in vivo results demonstrated that pCDPI can overcome the blood-brain barrier (BBB) and deeply penetrate into orthotopic glioma in mice, to inhibit IL-6-induced cell proliferation and achieve imaging-guided targeted drug delivery. Simultaneously, a pH-sensitive sustained release of doxorubicin (DOX) accompanied with real-time fluorescence monitoring was realized. A distinct synergistic therapeutic outcome could be achieved which suggests the presented nanomedicine having promising potential for future cancer treatments.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Polymer-coated carbon nanodots
Blood-brain barrier
Interleukin-6 receptor
Real-time monitoring
Theranostics

xocs:hub-sec.xocs:hub-sec-title: Biomaterials for the Delivery of Drugs, Genes, Vaccines and Active Molecules

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Drug loading capacity and drug release measurements
Real-time monitoring of drug release via FRET between DOX and pCDPI
Cellular uptake and in vivo targeting
In vitro and in vivo BBB transportation
Optical properties and real-time monitoring of dynamic drug release in vitro and in vivo
Glioma targeted drug delivery across the BBB

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the grants from National Natural Science Foundation of China (21675032), National Key Basic Research Program (2013CB932502) of China (973 Program), National Natural Science Foundation of China (81573002) and Sino-German Research Project (GZ995).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of (a) pCD and (b) pCDPI. The Insets in (a) and (b) were the DLS-measured hydrodynamic particle diameters of pCD and pCDPI, respectively. (c) HRTEM image of pCDPI, and the corresponding fast Fourier transform (FFT) image (inset). The yellow and orange lines circled the graphitic carbon core and the polymer coating, respectively, while the green lines represented the PEG and I6P8 conjugation. (d) Inverse FFT (IFFT) image of the graphitic carbon core. (e) Zeta potentials of different pCD-based nanoparticles. (f) In vitro cumulative release of free DOX or DOX from pCDPID in PBS with different pH values. (g, h, I and J) Changes in blue and red fluorescence upon increasing DOX/pCDPI concentrations ratios (FRET ON), upon DOX release from pCDPID in acidic PBS (pH = 5.0) for different time periods (FRET OFF), and upon DOX release from pCDPID in PBS with different pH values for 12 h, respectively. The images in g were corresponding optical images of fluorescence colour under different tests. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) Confocal images of U87 cells incubated with pCDPID for different time periods. The pCDPI channel (blue): λEx = 405 nm & λEm = 450–490 nm; DOX channel (red): λEx = 405 nm & λEm = 570–610 nm. Bar: 50 μm. (b) In vivo fluorescence images of a representative tumor-bearing mouse at different time points after intravenous injection of pCDPID. The pCDPI channel: λEx = 430 nm & λEm = 500 nm; DOX channel: λEx = 430 nm & λEm = 600 nm. The yellow lines circled the tumor area. (c) Quantified fluorescence intensity for images in “a” and the corresponding FRET curve (n = 3). (d) Quantified fluorescence intensity for tumor area in “b” and the corresponding FRET curve (n = 3). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) The permeability and transport ratio of pCDP and pCDPI across the in vitro BBB at various incubation time periods (n = 3). (b) Immunofluorescence images of brain sections from glioma-bearing mice injected with saline, pCDP and pCDPI, respectively. Blue: nanoparticles; red: RFP-U87 glioma cells; green: anti-CD31 labled blood vessels. yellow arrows: co-localization of nanoparticles and blood vessels; white arrows: co-localization of nanoparticles and glioma cells. Bar: 100 μm. (c) Confocal images of U87 and RA cells incubated with pCDP or pCDPI, respectively. Blue: pCD. Bar: 50 μm. (d) Quantified blue fluorescence intensity for images in “c” (n = 3). Notes: **p < 0.01; ***p < 0.001. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) In vivo fluorescence images of U87 tumor-bearing mice at different time intervals after intravenous injection of pCDPI or pCDP. Yellow lines circled the glioma area. (b) The ex vivo images of main organs and their corresponding fluorescence quantification data harvested at 4 h post injection (n = 3). (c) Fluorescence distribution of U87 glioma spheroids after incubation with pCDP and pCDPI for 4 h. From left to right: representative fluorescence images at the determined scanning layers, orthogonal images, and 3D fluorescence images. Blue: pCD. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) Cell cycle profiles of U87 cells after different treatments. (b) Optical images of U87 glioma spheroids after incubation with various formulations for different days. Bar: 200 μm. (c) Volume histograms of glioma spheroids and the corresponding cell propagations detected by CCK-8 after different treatments. (d) Cytotoxicity of U87 cells after treatment with different concentrations of free DOX, pCDPD and pCDPID, respectively (n = 3). Notes: *p < 0.05; **p < 0.01; ***p < 0.001.

(a) Tumor apoptosis of glioma-bearing mice treated with saline, pCDP, pCDPD, pCDPI, pCDPID and DOX, respectively. Blue: DAPI-stained cell nuclei; green: FITC-stained apoptotic cells. Yellow lines represented the glioma borders and the arrows pointed to the inner sides of glioma. (b) Average body weight and (c) Kaplan-Meier survival curves of glioma-bearing mice after different treatments (n = 8). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Schematic diagrams of in vivo delivery process of multifunctional pCDPID and its application for targeted imaging, pH-sensitive chemotherapy, real time monitoring and biotherapy.

head.ce:title: Augmented glioma-targeted theranostics using multifunctional polymer-coated carbon nanodots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutics, School of Pharmacy, Key Laboratory of Smart Drug Delivery, Ministry of Education, Fudan University, Shanghai 201203, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Smart Drug Delivery

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Center for Advanced Low-dimension Materials, Donghua University, Shanghai 201620, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Pharmaceutical Biotechnology, Center for System-based Drug Research, Center for Nanoscience (CeNS), Ludwig-Maximilians-Universität München, Munich 81377, Germany

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Neurosurgery, Affiliated Hospital of Nantong University, Nantong 226001, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: e
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Center of Analysis and Measurement, Fudan University, Shanghai 200433, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Department of Pharmaceutics, School of Pharmacy, Key Laboratory of Smart Drug Delivery, Ministry of Education, Fudan University, Shanghai 201203, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Smart Drug Delivery

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Center for Advanced Low-dimension Materials, Donghua University, Shanghai 201620, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Overcoming biological barriers to imaging-guided site-specific delivery of therapeutics is the goal of current nanomedicine designs. Here, multifunctional polymer-coated carbon nanodots with an interleukin-6 (IL-6) fragment peptide for receptor-targeting (pCDPI) were prepared for drug delivery. The pCDPI exhibits small hydrodynamic diameters, high water solubility and biocompatibility. In vitro and in vivo results demonstrated that pCDPI can overcome the blood-brain barrier (BBB) and deeply penetrate into orthotopic glioma in mice, to inhibit IL-6-induced cell proliferation and achieve imaging-guided targeted drug delivery. Simultaneously, a pH-sensitive sustained release of doxorubicin (DOX) accompanied with real-time fluorescence monitoring was realized. A distinct synergistic therapeutic outcome could be achieved which suggests the presented nanomedicine having promising potential for future cancer treatments.

ce:section.ce:para: Glioma has been considered to be the most malignant tumor within central nervous system with high morbidity and mortality [1]. Various biological barriers, especially the blood-brain barrier (BBB), greatly hinder the drug accumulation within diseased areas. Recently, nanoparticle-based drug delivery systems have emerged as suitable vehicles for cancer treatments due to the improved efficacy and reduced systemic toxicity compared with conventional drug formulations [2,3]. However, obstacles, such as the multidrug resistance, the nonspecific distribution of therapeutics and the absence of real-time dose monitoring, remain serious challenges for the development of brain tumor therapeutics [4,5]. An ideal nanoparticle-based drug delivery system for glioma treatment should be a simple construction with capability of: (1) prolonging the blood circulation lifetime of drugs and overcoming the BBB, (2) site-specific accumulation within and homogenous deep penetration into tumors, (3) modulating the controllable release profiles in response to exogenous/endogenous stimuli, (4) real-time visualization of drug dosing at the targeted sites, and (5) effective inhibition of tumor growth or killing the tumor cells via different therapeutic modes. Meanwhile, the smart nanoparticles should have small sizes, high water-solubility and high biocompatibility, which are beneficial for the post-clearance from the body without causing undue toxicity [6,7].

Carbon nanodots present a new class of small fluorescence nano-probes with non-blinking emission, high cell permeability and excellent biocompatibility for tumor imaging. Especially elaborate versions containing a π-framework have recently been designed to deliver chemotherapeutics of aromatic chemical structure such as doxorubicin (DOX) into tumor cells [8]. In addition, the sensitive Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) between the drug and those special carbon nanodots can be rationally exploited for real-time monitoring the dynamic release performance in cells. These characteristics suggest that carbon nanodots are promising candidates for drug development. Nonetheless, the small π-anchoring sites to the drug molecules and the limited functions of the current pristine carbon nanodots make them still far away from the ultimate theranostic outcomes [9]. Developing a simple construction of multifunctional carbon nanodots with capability of binding or encapsulating therapeutic cargos, simultaneously addressing all the above-mentioned issues for efficient glioma therapy in vitro and in vivo, is extremely desirable.

Polymer-coated carbon nanodots (pCD) with ultra-small size, high water solubility and high biocompatibility were synthesized by the direct solvothermal transformation as reported in our previous work [10]. They enabled glioma fluorescence imaging in vivo with good contrast based on tumoral delivery via the enhanced permeability and retention (EPR) effect. For active targeting, we focused on a peptide named I6P7, which is a fragment of interleukin-6 (IL-6) screened by phage display technique, which can specifically bind with the IL-6 receptor (IL-6R) [11]. IL-6R is expressed on both brain capillaries endothelial cells (essential key part of the BBB) and various malignant tumor cells including glioma cells [12,13]. This special peptide binding can also inhibit tumor growth in an antagonistic manner through blocking endogenous IL-6 interaction with IL-6R [11,14,15]. Therefore, in this work, the pCD were covalently conjugated with I6P8 (cys-I6P7) to construct a simple nanocarrier for augmenting the drug delivery beyond passive targeting. As shown here, this nanocarrier can stably encapsulate the drug DOX, overcome the BBB and deeply penetrate into the tumor. The pH-responsive glioma-targeted drug delivery combined with inhibition of IL-6-induced cell proliferation can be visualized by FRET imaging (Scheme 1 ).

1-Methyl-2-pyrrolidinone (NMP) was bought from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (China). α-Maleimidyl-ω-N-hydroxysuccinimidyl polyethylene glycol (NHS-PEG-MAL, MW 3500) was purchased from JenKem Technology Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). I6P8 peptide (N- to C-terminus: CLSLITRL) was synthesized by ZiYu Biotech Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). DOX was purchased from Huafeng United Technology Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). Primary rabbit anti-CD31 polyclonal antibody and Alexa Fluor®488-conjugated goat anti-rabbit IgG secondary antibody were obtained from Abcam (Cambridge, U.K.). Recombinant IL-6 was purchased from PeproTech Inc. (Princeton, USA). Cell cycle detection kit was ordered from the Beyotime Institute of Biotechnology. Cell Counting Kit-8 (CCK-8) was obtained from Dojindo Laboratories (Kumamoto, Japan). TdT-mediated dUTP nick end labeling (TUNEL) apoptosis detection kit (FITC-labeled) was purchased from KeyGEN Biotech (Nanjing, China). Other reagents, if not specified, were obtained from Gibco (Shanghai, China).

pCD were synthesized by one-step solvothermal reaction as described previously10. Briefly, 50 ml of NMP was heated in a 80 ml of Teflon-lined autoclave at 200 °C for above 10 h to form pCD, and then the obtained pCD were purified by vacuum distillation and dialysis (MWCO 500). To prepare PEG functionalized pCD (pCDP), pCD (1 mg) were darkly stirred with MAL-PEG-NHS (10 mg) in 1 ml of phosphate buffered solution (PBS, PH 8.0) for 2 h. Thereafter, cysteine mercapto group-containing I6P8 peptide (1.5 mg) was added and the pH of the solution was adjusted to 7.0. The mixture was stirred for 24 h in the dark and then the I6P8 peptide and PEG functionalized pCD (pCDPI) was recovered via dialysis (MWCO 5000) and lyophilization. DOX-loaded pCDPI was prepared by stirring DOX with the pCDPI in PBS (PH 7.4) at the mass ratio of 2:1 for overnight and purificaton by dialysis (MWCO 3500) to remove the loosely adsorbed DOX.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) were performed on a JEM-2100F instrument with an acceleration voltage of 200 kV. Zeta potential and hydrodynamic particle sizes were determined by Nano-ZS instrument (Malvern, UK). FTIR spectra were obtained on a Nicolet-670 spectrometer. 1H NMR spectra were recorded using a 400 MHz NMR spectrometer (Varian, USA). UV–vis spectra and fluorescence spectrum were conducted on UV-2401PC (Shimadzu, Australasia) and a Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer (Agilent, USA), respectively. Optical images were obtained on a Canon camera (Japan). Cell imaging was performed using a laser scanning confocal microscope (Carl Zeiss LSM710, Zeiss, Jena, Germany). In vivo and ex vivo fluorescent images were obtained by an In vivo IVIS spectrum imaging system (PerkinElmer, USA). Cell cycle was determined using a flow cytometry (FACSCalibur, BD, USA) and analyzed by Modfit software. TUNEL apoptosis was visualized using a DMI4000B inverted fluorescent microscope (Leica, Germany). The transendothelial electrical resistance (TEER) was monitored using an epithelial voltohmmeter (Millicell ERS®, Millipore, MA, USA).

Glioma U87 cells were incubated in a special Dulbecco's modified Eagle medium (DMEM) with the addition of 10% heat-inactivated fetal bovine serum (FBS), 1% l-glutamine, 1% penicillin and 1% streptomycin, and cultured at 37 °C in a 5% CO2 humid incubator. RFP-U87 cells were obtained by stably transfecting red fluorescence protein (RFP) gene into U87 cells and incubated under the unaltered conditions. Healthy astrocytes (RA cells) and brain capillary endothelial cells (bEnd.3 cells) were maintained under the same conditions as U87 cells except the 20% heat-inactivated FBS. The cells were seeded at a density of 1 × 105 cells/dish in confocal cell culture dish (GBS-35-20, NEST Biotech) for further experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Drug loading capacity and drug release measurements
ce:section.ce:para:
To study the drug loading capacity, 1 mg pCDPI was stirred in 1 ml DOX-PBS solution (PH 7.4, 2 mg/ml) overnight. Then, the unloaded DOX was removed by dialysis. The drug loading capacity was determined by dissolving pCDPI in deionized water and measuring the absorption at 488 nm. The DOX loading (mg/mg) was calculated as follows:
DOX loading (mg/mg) = weight of DOX loaded on pCDPI/weight of pCDPI

To study the pH-responsive drug release, the DOX loaded pCDPI (pCDPID) (1 ml, 100 μg/ml DOX) was dialyzed in different release media (20 ml PBS with pH 7.4, 6.0 and 5.0, respectively). The release curves were recorded via measuring the released DOX amount at predetermined time intervals, where 1 ml of release media were removed for measurement and an equal volume of fresh media were added.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Real-time monitoring of drug release via FRET between DOX and pCDPI
ce:section.ce:para: To illuminate the in vitro FRET effect, the FRET profiles of pCDPID subjected to different assays were recorded using fluorescence spectrometer with 400 nm excitation. To monitor the intracellular DOX release from pCDPID, U87 cells were incubated with pCDPID for different time periods (0.5, 12, 24, and 48 h). After washing with ice-cold PBS and fixing with 4% paraformaldehyde, they were examined under a laser scanning confocal microscope. The FRET donor pCD were excited at 405 nm and the fluorescence confocal images were done at two independent emission wavelength channels (450–490 nm for carbon nanodots channel and 570–610 nm for DOX channel). To monitor the in vivo drug release in tumor, the right flank of nude mice were injected subcutaneously with 100 μl of U87 cellular suspension (5 × 106 per mouse). When tumor reached appropriate size, pCDPID was injected into mouse via tail vein. Fluorescent imaging was obtained at various time points (1, 2, 4 and 10 h) at two independent emission wavelength channel (500 nm for carbon nanodots channel and 600 nm for DOX channel) using the In Vivo IVIS spectrum imaging system with 430 nm excitation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cellular uptake and in vivo targeting
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the concentration-dependent cellular uptake, U87 cells were incubated with different concentrations of pCDPI for 1 h at 37 °C. To investigate the energy-dependent cellular uptake, U87 cells were incubated with 100 μg/ml pCDPI (calculated with pCD, 1.5 ml) for 1 h at 37 °C and at 4 °C, respectively. To explore the I6P8 peptide-mediated cellular uptake, U87 cells were pre-incubated with I6P8 peptide for 20 min, followed by the incubation with pCDPI for another 1 h at 37 °C. To explore the targeting cellular uptake, U87 cells and RA cells were incubated with the same concentrations (100 μg/ml, 1.5 ml) of pCDP or pCDPI, respectively, at 37 °C for 1 h. All these cultured cells were immobilized and then observed under laser scanning confocal microscope. To evaluate the in vivo targeting effect, U87 orthotopic glioma model was established by injection of U87 cells (5 × 105 cells in 5 μl Hank's solution) into the right corpus striatum of nude mouse using a stereotactic fixation device with a mouse adaptor. Twenty days after implantation, the glioma-bearing mice were intravenously injected with pCDP and pCDPI, respectively, at a dose of 1 mg pCD/mouse. Fluorescence images were then obtained at various time points (1, 2, 3 and 4 h) after injection using the In Vivo IVIS spectrum imaging system. At 4 h post injection, the mice were sacrificed and all major organs including brains were removed carefully to capture ex vivo fluorescence imaging.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro and in vivo BBB transportation
ce:section.ce:para:
To study the BBB transportation, in vitro BBB model was builded. Typically, bEnd.3 cells were plated into polycarbonate 24-well transwell filters (Falcon Cell Culture Insert, Becton Dickinson Labware, NJ, USA) at a density of 4 × 104 cells/cm2 and cultured for seven days. Then, transendothelial electrical resistance (TEER) was measured to exam the tightness of the formed in vitro BBB model (Scheme S2). Those models with TEER values of above 200 Ω cm2 were incubated separately with pCDP or pCDPI (100 μg/ml) at 37 °C on a shaking platform. Simultaneously, fluorescein sodium (NaF, final concentration at 10 μg/ml) was added to monitor the integrity of BBB model during the whole experiment. At 10, 15, 30, 45, and 60 min time intervals, 400 μl of the culture solution was removed from each acceptor chamber for the measurements, and immediately replaced with 400 μl fresh medium. The transport ratio of carriers was evaluated via fluorescent quantitation using a fluorescence spectrophotometer and calculated according to the formula: transport ratio (%) = Wn/W × 100, where Wn is the amount of samples taken from acceptor chamber at the n-th minute (n = 10, 15, 30, 45, 60), and W is the total amount of samples in donor chamber. The apparent permeability was calculated as follows:
P
a p p
=
∂ Q
∂ t ·
C 0
· A
where
∂ Q / ∂ t
is the permeability rate (μg/s), C 0 is the initial concentration (μg/ml) of samples in the donor chamber, and A is the surface area (cm2) of the membrane filter.

To study the in vivo BBB transportation, RFP-U87 orthotopic glioma model was established by inoculating with RFP-U87 cells. Twenty days after inoculation, NaCl, pCDP or pCDPI were separately injected into glioma-bearing mice via tail vein at dose of 1 mg pCD/mouse. Two hours later, the brains of the sacrificed mice were extracted to prepare frozen sections (20 μm). The blood vessels were stained with primary rabbit anti-CD31 polyclonal antibody and then Alexa Fluor®488-conjugated goat anti-rabbit IgG secondary antibody according to the instructions. The co-localized fluorescent images were obtained using confocal microscope.

To investigate the tumor penetration, U87 cells (4 × 103/400 μL per well) were cultured in 2% (w/v) sterile low-melting-point agarose-coated 48-well plates for approximately seven days to form a three-dimensional glioma spheroid. Then, pCDP and pCDPI (100 μg/ml) were separately incubated with the glioma spheroid for 4 h. After rinse and immobilization, the glioma spheroids were scanned every 10 μm from top to bottom using laser scanning confocal microscope (Ex: 405 nm).

Cell numbers analysis, glioma spheroids growth, and cell cycle measurement were all used to study the cell proliferation inhibition. Typically, (1) Cell numbers analysis: U87 cells (2 × 103/well) were cultured in 96-well plates for 24 h, followed by starvation for another 24 h via incubating in serum-free medium. After synchronization, I6P8 peptide, pCDPI or pCDP were separately incubated with the cells for 20 min. Then, recombinant IL-6 with a final concentration of 100 ng/ml was added. After continual incubation for a determined time periods (1, 2, or 3 days), 10 μL CCK-8 regent was put into the well to incubate for another 1 h. The absorbance at 450 nm which reported the live cell numbers was recorded using a microplate reader. Here, the cells without any treatment or with only 100 ng/ml IL-6 incubation were used as controls. (2) Glioma spheroids growth: Three-dimensional glioma spheroids were obtained according to the above description. The spheroids with similar size were incubated with serum-free media containing I6P8 peptide, pCDPI or pCDP, respectively. After 20 min, recombinant IL-6 with a final concentration of 100 ng/ml was added into the wells. After continual incubation for 1, 3 or 5 days, these spheroids were imaged using an inverted fluorescence microscope. Simultaneously, their volume was calculated using the following formula: V = π × a × b2/6, where, a and b are the longest and shortest diameters of each glioma spheroid, respectively. Here, the spheroids without any treatment or with only 100 ng/ml IL-6 incubation were used as controls. (3) Cell cycle measurements: U87 cells that treated according to the above protocol (1) were recovered via washing with pre-cold PBS and immobilization in 75% pre-cold ethanol at 4 °C for 18 h. Then, the immobilized cells were stained with propidium iodide for 30 min according to the instructions. The cell populations at different phases were acquired using a flow cytometry.

U87 cells were incubated with pCDP, pCDPI, free DOX, pCDD, pCDPD or pCDPID at determined concentrations for 24 h, respectively. Then, culture media were refreshed and 10 μl CCK-8 solution was added for another 2 h incubation. The absorbance of each well at 450 nm was measured by a microplate reader to report the cytotoxicity. Here, U87 cells without any treatment were used as control.

To evaluate the in vivo anti-glioma activity of nanoparticles, U87 orthotopic glioma was implanted as above. Then, the glioma-bearing mice were randomly and equally divided into six groups, and intravenously injected with 100 μl of physiological saline, pCDP, pCDPI, DOX, pCDPD and pCDPID, respectively, at a dose of 5 mg/kg DOX-equiv. The injection was performed every 3 days with a total of 3 doses per nude mouse. At the third day, a part of mice were sacrificed and their brains were extracted to prepare the DAPI-stained frozen sections (20 μm) for apoptotic cells detection via TUNEL apoptosis detection kit (FITC-labeled). At the same time, paraffin-embedded brains were cut into 4 μm sections for H&E staining. Finally, all sections were observed under a DMI4000B inverted fluorescent microscope. The other part mice (8 mice of each group) were used to calculate the survival time.

Fluorescence quantifications for confocal images of cells were done on system software (ZEN 2012) of Leica confocal microscopy. Three representative images were used to quantify the mean fluorescence intensity. Fluorescence quantifications for the images of in vivo tumor and ex organs were obtained by In Vivo IVIS spectrum imaging software. The regions of interest were elaborately selected before the quantifications.
All data were expressed as mean ± S.D. Survival time was recorded and analyzed by GraphPad Prism 5. The statistical difference was performed via one way analysis of variance (ANOVA).

First, pCD were synthesized via direct solvothermal transformation of 1-methyl-2-pyrrolidinone (NMP). NMP presents both the carbon source and nitrogen source, to produce polymer-coated nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots (see Materials and methods and Ref. [10]). TEM image in Fig. 1 a shows the small-sized nanodots (5–10 nm) with graphitic cores and polymer shells. 1H NMR spectra, FTIR spectra, DLS and water dispersity proofed a hydrophilic polymer surface containing amines (Fig. 1a and Fig. S1). Then, pCDPI were prepared via reaction with bifunctional PEG (NHS-PEG3400-MAL) which bridges pCD with the I6P8 peptide (Scheme S1). TEM images revealed the preservation of graphitic cores@polymer shells structure after this modification (Fig. 1b–d and Fig. S2a–c), while 1H NMR and FTIR spectra proved that this PEG/I6P8-modification were based on the specific covalent reactions of pCD amino groups with PEG NHS active ester and PEG maleimide with the N-terminal cysteine mercapto group of I6P8, respectively (Fig. S1). Zeta potential results showed a gradually elevation from −32.9 mV of pCD to −13.2 mV of pCDPI, which could be attributed to the shielding effect of PEGylation [16] and a slight positive charge of I6P8 peptide (Fig. 1e). DLS curves demonstrated the most probable particle diameters of 17 nm, 28 nm, and 33 nm for pCD, pCDP and pCDPI, respectively, only slightly larger than values obtaining in TEM measurements due to the hydrated polymer coatings, which confirmed the monodispersity of all nanoparticles in water (Fig. 1a–b and Fig. S2d). Although the hydration radius increased after PEGylation and I6P8 modification, their transparent appearance without detectable sedimentation (under a super centrifugation of 10000 rpm for 10 min) confirmed their high water-solubility and stability in different physiological media (Fig. S3). This property together with the optimal small size of pCDPI was considered as beneficial for a long circulation of nanoparticle-formulated drugs in vivo and the ability to extravasate to disease sites on the one hand, and an improved safety and tolerability on the other hand.

The graphitic cores of pCDPI facilitate the loading of aromatic anticancer drug DOX via π-π stacking [17]. This loading could be stabilized by the polymer coating with increased thickness. As measured by UV–vis spectroscopy (Fig. S4), the optimized DOX loading amount on pCDPI was 0.28 ± 0.01 mg/mg, higher than those of reported carbon nanodots [18,19]. As revealed by the release profiles (Fig. 1f), a low cumulated release (13.1%) at pH 7.4 over the course (48 h) suggested the stable encapsulation of DOX into pCDPI under physiological condition. Fortunately, the release was pH-responsive with significant increase in acidic solutions, where the highest one over 48 h could reach 68.5% at pH 5.0. This pH-sensitive release performance was attributed to the acid-induced protonation of NH2 groups in DOX, thereby reducing the hydrophobic π-π interaction between DOX and graphitic cores [20,21], which was similar to those of other reported carbon-based drug vehicles such as graphene and carbon nanotubes [22,23]. The elevated release at low pH is favorable for cancer therapy, because of the minimized drug leakage in neutral environment of off-target sites and the maximal targeting drug accumulation in acidic cancer/tumor and intracellular endo-lysosomal microenvironment. In addition, pCDPI could also mediate the sustained drug release over a long period (>48 h) as compared with almost complete release (about 12 h) of free DOX molecules.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties and real-time monitoring of dynamic drug release in vitro and in vivo
ce:section.ce:para: Owing to the quantum effect and special surface state [24,25], pCDPI possessed unique optical performances. As exhibited in Fig. S5a–b, pCDPI exhibited a typical excitation- and concentration-dependent fluorescence emission similar to those of pristine carbon nanodots [26]. Upon excitation at 400 nm, the optimal emission was centered at about 475 nm. Its intensity was almost identical with the pCDP solution, but a slightly higher than its unmodified counterparts (Fig. S5c), possibly due to the increased hydrophilicity and consequently the decreased fluorescence quenching after the polymer coating. This result suggested the potential of pCDPI in tumor fluorescence imaging. Interestingly, the strong emission (475 nm) of pCDPI upon excitation (400 nm) was approximately located at the optimal absorption (excitation) of DOX for its red fluorescence (590 nm) (Fig. S5d), which therefore could be exploited to monitor the DOX release from pCDPI via the distance-sensitive FRET effect. As shown in Fig. 1h, the increase of DOX loading amount on pCDPI caused a gradually decreased blue-green emission of nanocarrier and an increased red emission of DOX, where the relative fluorescence intensity ratios (I DOX/I pCDPI) was linear with DOX/pCDPI concentration ratio from 0 to 0.8 (Fig. S6). At DOX/pCDPI concentration ratio of 0.8:1, the relative fluorescence intensity ratio (I DOX/I pCDPI) reached a maximum value of 1.80, suggesting a saturation absorption of DOX on pCDPI. Subsequently, the DOX loaded pCDPI was subjected to slightly acidic conditions (pH = 5.0), which triggered a time-dependent recovery of blue-green emission accompanied by the decrease of DOX fluorescence (Fig. 1i). The finding indicates a gradual drug release at physiological acidic solution present in the tumor cell micro-environment, where the separation between pCDPI and DOX created a decreased FRET signal change. Moreover, the analogous phenomenon was observed with the decrease of pH from 7.4 to 5.0 (Fig. 1j), further demonstrating the pH-responsive release behavior. This was verified in Fig. 1g, where the increase of the DOX loading amount on pCDPI resulted in an obvious eye-visible change of the color from blue-green to red. It was quite the contrary when the pH of solution was decreased or the release time was prolonged. According to these results, the sensitive FRET signal change might be feasible to real-time monitor the dynamic drug release.

To shed light on this observation, the drug release was monitored in real-time on U87 glioma cells using confocal fluorescence microscopy. As shown in Fig. 2 a, both blue and red fluorescence was observed at all the incubation time points, corresponding to the emission of pCDPI nanodots and the FRET signal from nanodots to DOX, respectively. At the beginning (0.5 h incubation), a relatively weak blue signal and a strong red signal suggested s high FRET, which is consistent with the close proximity (no obvious release) between the nanodots and DOX. With the increase of incubation time from 0.5 to 48 h, a substantial increase of the blue fluorescence was observed accompanied with a decrease of red fluorescence intensity, indicating a compromised FRET corresponding to the gradual release of DOX from the nanodots. This performance could be quantified (Fig. 2c). In the parallel control experiment, the incubation of non-loaded pCDPI carrier with glioma cells produce negligible FRET signal (Fig. S7). Thus, the intracellular drug release process can be monitored real-timely by measuring the FRET signal change using confocal microscopy. Moreover, this FRET monitoring of drug release can be performed also in vivo in tumors by using a bioimaging system, where the gradually decreased FRET signals along with the time after intravenous injection were clearly visible in the tumor region (Fig. 2b and d). Therefore, pCDPID presents reliable nano-encapsulation form with the integrated option of real-time dose monitoring.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Glioma targeted drug delivery across the BBB
ce:section.ce:para: Because of the small size, high water solubility and polymer coating as well as the I6P8 targeting peptide, pCDPI might display favorable BBB penetration and glioma targeting. As shown in the in vitro BBB monolayer results in Fig. 3 a, the decreased apparent permeability coefficient (Papp) and the increased transport ratio suggested the gradual penetration of pCDP across the BBB monolayer during 60 min incubation. This transport could be elevated by the I6P8 conjugation to pCDP. The TEER value of BBB monolayer and the permeability of NaF were not significantly different from those of control during the experiment, indicating that the pCDPI might cross the BBB via transcellular but not paracellular pathway. In vivo delivery results showed that pCDP mostly colocalized with BBB blood vessels, and partially accumulated in glioma cells, possibly due to a passive EPR effect (Fig. 3b). In contrast, after I6P8 conjugation, much more pCDPI penetrated across the BBB blood vessels to accumulate in glioma, confirming the I6P8-mediated specific binding with tumor neovasculature and, subsequent to extravasation, a targeting effect into tumor parenchyma, probably via the high IL-6R expression.

In sum, after penetrating the BBB, pCDPI can specifically accumulate in glioma cells via I6P8 peptide-mediated targeting. As evaluated by confocal microscopy (Fig. 3c–d), the conjugation of I6P8 peptide could significantly enhance the cellular uptake of the carbon nanodots in U87 glioma cells, but had no apparent difference in normal astrocyte cells (RA cells). Furthermore, the cellular uptake of pCDPI in U87 glioma cells was obviously higher than that in normal healthy RA astrocytes. In contrast, there was no remarkable difference for the uptake of unconjugated nanoparticles (pCDP) in these two cell lines (Fig. 3c–d). Pre-incubation with excessive I6P8 peptide could competitively block the receptor binding of pCDPI with glioma cells, resulting in a reduced cellular uptake (Fig. S8). Moreover, the cellular uptake of pCDPI was energy- and concentration-dependent as evidenced by the enhanced fluorescence under higher temperature and concentration, respectively (Figs. S8 and S9). In addition, the apparent fluorescence signals at 1 h incubation in the merged confocal 2.5 D topological images confirmed the advanced cellular uptake of pCDPI. These results revealed the glioma-targeting ability of pCDPI nanoparticles, where the specific binding of I6P8 peptide to the glioma cell-overexpressed IL-6R was the main targeting mechanism.

The glioma targeting ability was further evaluated on tumor-bearing nude mice using an in vivo imaging system. As demonstrated in Fig. 4 a, intravenous injection of pCDPI and pCDP both enabled the fluorescence-based tumor imaging due to an elevated EPR-mediated accumulation. However, the I6P8 conjugation resulted in the more intensive fluorescence in the tumor region, indicating the improved in vivo glioma targeting effect via I6P8 mediation. Moreover, I6P8 conjugation led to a long accumulation of the nanoparticles in the tumor as observed from the unfading fluorescence during 4 h post injection. Differently, pCDP showed an extravasation from the tumor, possibly due to the drug efflux pumps [27]. These results further exhibited the enhanced targeting accumulation ability of pCDPI. Notably, besides the prolonged accumulation of these nanoparticles in tumor, their retention within the liver and specifically the kidney (Fig. 4b) implied that they might be eliminated by these two organs. This manner is beneficial for practical clinical application.

Besides the BBB and the targeting accumulation, the poor drug penetration into solid tumors caused by fibrillar collagen of extracellular matrix and elevated interstitial fluid pressure is another biological barrier that a nano-drug must overcome [28,29]. Hence, glioma tumor spheroids were used to evaluate the tumor penetration ability of pCDPI. As revealed by the fluorescence distribution in Fig. 4c, the gradually decreased fluorescence intensity from shell to core suggested the relative rigidity of solid tumor and therefore the difficulty for drug's access. Nevertheless, both the as-prepared formulations here could penetrate into relatively deep sections of glioma spheroids, due to their small size and the hydrophilic polymer coating. Comparatively, the I6P8-conjugated formulation mediated much deeper fluorescence signals than the others, suggesting that I6P8 could synergistically enhance the tumor spheroid penetration.

It has been reported that I6P8 peptide can block the IL-6-mediated pathway via its specific binding with IL-6R receptor [11]. Thus pCDPI was expected to inhibit the tumor growth. As shown in Fig. 5 a, the natural ligand IL-6 promoted the cell proliferation from S phase to G2/M phase. After treatment by pCDPI, the G2/M phase distinctly decreased while the G1 phase increased, suggesting growth inhibition of glioma cells. This inhibition could be quantified by measuring the volumes of tumor spheroids after different treatments. As indicated in Fig. 5b–c, the IL-6-treated glioma spheroids grew faster and the volumes were about 1.12-, 1.50- and 1.86-fold of that of the control group on day 1, 3 and 5, respectively. Whereas, I6P8 formulations-pretreated spheroids experienced significant delay in growth. This inhibitory effects of pCDPI on the glioma growth was validated by a CCK-8 assay (Fig. 5c). These results demonstrated that I6P8 peptide still maintained its antitumor activity after being conjugated to pCD. Because of the existence of endogenous IL-6 in vivo, especially in cancerous patients [30,31], the pCDPI-triggered inhibition of tumor growth by blocking the oncogenic function of IL-6 is considered to be important for cancer therapy.

As the result of the enhanced drug delivery, pCDPID demonstrated a synergistic therapeutic outcome. As shown in Fig. S11, pCDP nanocarrier exhibited negligible cytotoxicity even at 800 μg/ml, suggesting its high biocompatibility. After DOX loading, both the targeted and non-targeted pCD-based nano-drugs exhibited obvious cytotoxicity, which was higher than that of pure DOX at the same drug concentration (Fig. 5d). These results are consistent with the efficacious cellular uptake of DOX assisted by pCD-based nano-vehicles. Moreover, I6P8 containing pCDPID had an enhanced cytotoxicity as compared with I6P8-free DOX loaded pCDP (pCDPD), validating the peptide-mediated targeted delivery. Further assessment of the in vivo anti-glioma efficacy is presented in Fig. 6 . The pCDP only nano-carrier had high biocompatibility and did not result in apparent glioma apoptosis, similar to the saline treatment (Fig. 6a). Whereas, DOX-loaded nanoparticles caused noticeable apoptosis of glioma cells (Fig. 6a) and obvious inhibition in the tumor growth (Fig. S13), which could be increased by I6P8 conjugation to the nano-drug. This confirms the cytotoxicity results. Interestingly, intravenous injection of pCDPI can also induce glioma apoptosis. This might be attributed to the overexpression of glioma cell growth factor IL-6 in vivo [32,33] and blockade of IL-6-mediated tumor growth by pCDPI as explained above (Fig. 5). In addition, body weights and the survival time of glioma-bearing mice treated by different nano-formulations were recorded to monitor the therapeutic effects as well as potential adverse reactions (Fig. 6b–c). As indicated in Fig. 6, although pure DOX treatment could slightly prolong the median survival time compared to the saline and pCDP treatments, the fast loss of weight of treated mice reflects severe side effects. Delivered by pCD-based nanoparticles in a rather passive EPR-based mode, pCDPD displayed improved chemotherapeutic effects presumably also promoted by the pH-triggered drug release in acidic tumor microenvironment. Most important, the IL-6R targeted biotherapies pCDPI and pCDPID displayed the most favorable therapeutic effects at the lowest weight loss. For reasons as explained above, the DOX-free pCDPI prolong the median survival time with only moderate weight loss. The pCDPID treatment, involving both I6P8-mediated targeting/tumor growth inhibition and pH-sensitive DOX delivery with deep tumor penetration, resulted in minimal weight loss and the increased median survival time of 54 days, much longer than those of the other treatments. Notably, endogenous IL-6 was reported to be involved in resistance of cancer cells to the chemotherapeutic drugs [34], thus the inhibition of IL-6 via I6P8-conjugated nano-formulations might enhance the sensitivity towards DOX chemotherapy. In sum, these in vivo results illuminate a synergistic targeted chemo-biotherapy effect of pCDPID, suggesting a high potential for practical cancer treatment.

Most of current strategies for delivery vehicles, including biomolecules, polymer nanoparticles, inorganic nanoparticles and their derivations, often suffer from low stability, poor tumor-specific targeting, severe toxicity or compromised therapeutic effect [35,36]. In this study, we developed multifunctional polymer-coated carbon nanodots for targeted and bioresponsive drug delivery (Scheme 1). This kind of nanodots possesses a number of advantages over currently existing drug delivery vehicles, as demonstrated by the in vitro and in vivo results. First, the nanodots are the intermediate phase from polymer to inorganic nanoparticles via Ostwald ripening [10,37], and combine both the high water solubility of polymers and the good stability of inorganic nanoparticles. Meanwhile, the drug DOX can be effectively adsorbed on nanodots' graphitic cores via π-π stacking and stabilized by the polymer coating. Because of the special interaction between DOX and nanodots, pH-responsive sustained-release can be achieved. Second, just as some carbon nanodots utilized to directly target glioma [38,39], this kind of nanodots has unique optical properties and therefore can be used as fluorescence probes for cancer imaging in vitro and in vivo. Moreover, the sensitive FRET between the nanodots and the drug can be exploited for monitoring real-time dynamic drug release. Third, the small size and hydrophilic polymer coating guarantees a synergistic dynamic balance between the in vivo retention and glioma accumulation. The medium-small size can induce the renal and hepatic clearance of the nanodots from the body without obvious retention [40,41], therefore reducing the risk of toxicity. The polymer coating provides the nanodots with longer circulation in the bloodstream, and increased opportunity to target the cancerous areas via EPR effect [42]. Most importantly, the site-specific accumulation of the nanodots can be further enhanced via the I6P8 peptide-targeting capacity. Fourth, attributing to the small size of the nanodots and the overexpressed IL-6R on the BBB and glioma cells, pCDPI can effectively cross the BBB and target the tumor with deep penetration. Fifth, the utilization of pCDPI to block the oncogenic function of IL-6 and consequently inhibit the tumor growth considerably broadens the range of cancer therapy. Assisted by the controllable chemotherapy, a synergistic therapeutic outcome augment the nanomedicine to treat many CNS diseases.

In summary, a multifunctional pCDPI was synthesized for the synergistic targeted drug delivery to glioma. Due to the small size, high water solubility and I6P8 peptide mediated targeting, the pCDPI nanodots can overcome the BBB and preferentially accumulate and deeply penetrate into the glioma. Via blocking the oncogenic function of IL-6, pCDPI could also inhibit the glioma growth and enhance the sensitivity of tumor cells towards chemotherapy. Attributing to the special π-π interaction and the sensitive FRET between DOX and pCDPI, the DOX loaded nanoparticles exhibited a pH-responsive sustained release manner and real-time dynamic drug release monitoring ability. Involving these unique functions and the high biocompatibility, pCDPID is expected to be a potential nanomedicine for practical clinical translation.
The authors declare no conflict of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the grants from National Natural Science Foundation of China (21675032), National Key Basic Research Program (2013CB932502) of China (973 Program), National Natural Science Foundation of China (81573002) and Sino-German Research Project (GZ995).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is the supplementary data related to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.biomaterials.2017.05.040.
